title: Prefix Sums (Range Sum Query)
code: private readonly int[] prefix;   // prefix[k] = sum nums[0..k-1]
public int SumRange(int left, int right) => prefix[right + 1] - prefix[left];

frames.caption: Allocate prefix with length n+1 = 7 and the sentinel prefix[0] = 0 (the sum "before index 0").
frames.note: The cleanest convention uses a prefix array one longer than nums, with a leading zero. That sentinel defines the empty-prefix total, which is what lets the query formula prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] work for every range — including ones starting at i = 0 — with no special case. Spend O(n) here once and every later range sum becomes a single O(1) subtraction.

frames.caption: prefix[1] = prefix[0] + nums[0] = 0 + 3 = 3.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: prefix[2] = prefix[1] + nums[1] = 3 + 1 = 4.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: prefix[3] = prefix[2] + nums[2] = 4 + 4 = 8.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: prefix[4] = prefix[3] + nums[3] = 8 + 1 = 9.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: prefix[5] = prefix[4] + nums[4] = 9 + 5 = 14.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: prefix[6] = prefix[5] + nums[5] = 14 + 9 = 23.
frames.note: The build recurrence is prefix[k] = prefix[k-1] + nums[k-1]: each entry extends the previous running total by exactly one more element. Because every entry does O(1) work and we sweep once, the whole prefix array is built in O(n). Note the index shift — prefix[k] summarizes nums[0..k-1], so nums[k-1] (not nums[k]) is the element folded in at step k. That one-off shift is the source of nearly every prefix-sum bug.

frames.caption: Prefix array is complete: [0, 3, 4, 8, 9, 14, 23]. Each prefix[k] is the running total of the first k elements.
frames.note: This is the "precompute once, query many" payoff: building cost O(n) up front turns an unlimited number of later range-sum queries into O(1) each. For q queries the total is O(n + q) instead of the naive O(n·q) of re-summing every range. The same precomputed array answers ANY range, not just one.

frames.caption: Query SumRange(1, 3): the inclusive sum of nums[1..3] = {1, 4, 1}.
frames.note: SumRange uses inclusive bounds, so the answer covers both ends. The formula is prefix[right+1] - prefix[left]. The +1 on right is the single most important detail: prefix is shifted one slot past nums, so to include nums[right] you must reach prefix[right+1]. Using prefix[right] instead silently drops the last element — the classic off-by-one.

frames.caption: Read the two prefix cells: prefix[3+1] = prefix[4] = 9 and prefix[1] = 3.
frames.note: Only two cells matter. prefix[right+1] is the total of everything up to and including index right; prefix[left] is the total of everything strictly before index left. Their difference is exactly the slice in between. No loop, no re-summing — two lookups regardless of how wide the range is, which is what makes the query O(1).

frames.caption: Subtract: prefix[4] - prefix[1] = 9 - 3 = 6. (Check: 1 + 4 + 1 = 6.)
frames.note: The subtraction cancels the shared prefix nums[0..left-1] and leaves precisely nums[left..right]. This works with negatives too — unlike a sliding window, prefix sums make no monotonicity assumption, the subtraction is exact for any signs. The same machinery inverts into the difference array: to apply many RANGE UPDATES cheaply you record diff[l] += v and diff[r+1] -= v, then one prefix-sum pass materializes the result. Prefix sum and difference array are exact inverses.
